authoringProvenance.author: OpenAI GPT-5.6 Pro — unreviewed candidate generation

items.prompt: An outer loop advances right from 0 to n−1. Inside it, a while loop advances left, but left never moves backward and advances at most n times total. What is the total time complexity of the boundary movement?
constraints: Each right increment and each left increment performs O(1) maintained-state work.
No helper scans the current window.

feedback.reason: The nested while loop is amortized over monotone pointer movement: right advances n times and left advances at most n times.
feedback.details: Syntactic nesting does not multiply iteration counts when the inner pointer keeps its progress across outer iterations. The outer loop contributes n right moves. Across the entire run, left can cross each index once, contributing at most n additional moves. With O(1) state updates per move, total work is O(n+n)=O(n). The bound would change if left reset for each right or if a validity check rescanned the window. The proof counts total operations over the complete execution rather than multiplying the maximum local loop lengths suggested by the source-code indentation.

items.prompt: A longest at-most-K-distinct window uses a hash map of frequencies. Assume expected O(1) hash operations and no more than K+1 keys during a transient violation. Select its expected time and extra-space complexities.
constraints: The map stores only keys currently represented in the window and removes zero-count keys.

feedback.reason: Monotone boundary movement gives expected O(n) time, while the active frequency map contains O(K) distinct keys.
feedback.details: Each element enters once and leaves at most once, so the number of expected constant-time map operations is linear. After repair there are at most K keys; immediately after adding a new distinct key there may be K+1, which is still O(K). Thus extra state is O(K), bounded by O(min(n, alphabetSize)) in concrete domains. If the alphabet is a fixed constant, the asymptotic space can be described as O(1), but under variable K the declared contract is O(K).

items.prompt: There are n−k+1 fixed-length windows. An implementation recomputes each window sum by visiting all k elements instead of using a rolling update. What is its time complexity?
constraints: No prefix sums or rolling state are used.

feedback.reason: The implementation performs Θ(k) work for each of Θ(n) candidate starts, giving O(nk).
feedback.details: The exact operation count is proportional to (n−k+1)·k. In two-parameter notation this is O(nk), and when k grows with n it can become quadratic. The fixed-window pattern is linear only when overlap is reused through O(1) add/remove updates, or when an equivalent prefix-state query gives O(1) per range. Merely using left and right indices does not make the implementation O(n). If k is an externally fixed constant the expression simplifies to O(n), but the general input-parameter contract must retain k in the bound.

items.prompt: A frequency window is described as O(n) because each map update is O(1). Which statement is the precise complexity qualification?
constraints: The implementation uses a general-purpose hash map.
The analysis distinguishes expected from adversarial worst-case behaviour.

options.text: The traversal is expected O(n) under expected O(1) hash operations; a pathological hash-map worst case can be worse unless the map contract provides stronger bounds.
Hash lookup is worst-case O(1), so the algorithm is unconditionally worst-case O(n).
Using any map automatically makes each operation O(log n), so the algorithm is O(n log n).
The shrink loop makes the algorithm O(n²) regardless of map cost.

feedback.reason: The pointer count is linear, but the total bound inherits the operation guarantee of the chosen frequency structure.
feedback.details: The algorithm performs O(n) state operations because each element enters and leaves at most once. With an ordinary hash map, those operations are commonly analyzed as expected O(1), yielding expected O(n) time. A balanced ordered map would instead give O(n log K), while a fixed-array alphabet can provide deterministic O(1) updates. Claiming unconditional worst-case linear time requires a data-structure contract that supports it. The nested loop alone does not cause quadratic work.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.worst_case_always_linear: General hash tables do not universally promise worst-case constant-time operations under all collision patterns.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.map_makes_nlogn: Ordered maps often cost logarithmic time, but hash maps are normally expected constant time; the structure must be named precisely.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.nested_means_n2: Left does not restart, so its total moves remain O(n); only the per-operation state cost changes the bound.

items.prompt: Both boundaries move only forward, but after each right step the code scans every element currently in the window to recompute whether it is valid. What can happen to the time complexity?
constraints: Window length can be Θ(n) for Θ(n) different right positions.
The full-window scan is separate from pointer movement.

options.text: It can be O(n²) because Θ(n) right steps may each perform a Θ(n) validity scan.
It remains O(n) solely because left and right are monotone.
A validity predicate over a window is automatically O(log n).
If left rarely moves, the repeated scans become O(1).

feedback.reason: Monotone boundaries bound pointer moves, not hidden work that repeatedly traverses the interior of the current range.
feedback.details: The usual O(n) proof assumes O(1) or otherwise bounded state maintenance per element entering or leaving. If each outer iteration rescans a window that can contain Θ(n) elements, the sum of scan lengths can be Θ(n²), even when left never moves backward. Rare shrinking can make this worse by keeping windows large. To recover linear or near-linear time, maintain the validity-driving statistic incrementally or use a structure with a stated update/query cost.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_linear: Pointer monotonicity accounts only for boundary operations; it does not amortize independent full-window scans.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_logarithmic: Predicate cost depends on the data structure and implementation; no logarithmic guarantee is automatic.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant_if_no_shrink: A stationary left boundary leaves a growing window, so rescanning it can cost more, not less.
